metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC#3) that mints a JWT with an `scp` claim (no `scope` claim) and asserts the returned `AccessToken.scopes` are correctly populated, covering the `scp`→scopes mapping path.
evidence: 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes' — What section
AC#3: 'an `AccessToken` with the expected scopes' — no mention of which claim or a separate `scp`-specific case
No AC explicitly mints a JWT with an `scp` claim and asserts the scopes are correctly extracted
findings.finding: The plan body (What section) describes that the verifier 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes' as a deliverable, but no AC explicitly verifies the `scp` claim path. AC#3 (happy-path) only requires 'expected scopes' without specifying which claim is used, and none of the negative-control ACs cover `scp`. If the implementation omits `scp` mapping, no test would fail.
findings.location: What section ('maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes') vs Acceptance Criteria #3 (happy-path unit test)
scenarios: An implementation that only reads the `scope` claim passes all ACs but silently drops scopes for providers (e.g. Azure AD / Entra ID) that emit `scp` instead of `scope`.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC#3 with a second case: 'and, in a parallel case, a JWT whose scopes are carried in the `scp` claim (no `scope` claim present) also yields the correct scopes in `AccessToken.scopes`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test asserting that a valid JWT with neither `scope` nor `scp` claim (scope-less token) is accepted and produces `AccessToken.scopes == []` (empty list, not an error), covering the 'empty path' variant of the scope/scp mapping.
evidence: Plan states: 'maps scope/scp→scopes' as a first-class behavior
AC3 specifies 'AccessToken with the expected scopes' but only in the happy path — no AC covers a token where neither scope nor scp is present
ABSENCE: no AC tests scope=[] / scp absent → AccessToken.scopes == [] (empty but not an error)
RFC 6749 §3.3: scope is OPTIONAL in access tokens; many machine-to-machine flows issue scope-less tokens
findings.finding: The scope/scp claim mapping — explicitly specified behavior ('maps scope/scp→scopes') — has no test for the empty/absent case: a well-formed token carrying no scope or scp claim. For a verifier targeting a 'wide variety of environments,' a token with no scope claim is a valid OAuth access token (scope is optional per RFC 6749), and if the verifier throws or mishandles the absent claim, every scope-less client is silently broken. The rubric's 'empty path' variant of a new user-facing flow is unaddressed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (happy path scopes) and the 'maps scope/scp→scopes' clause in the What section
scenarios: M2M client credential flow tokens often carry no scope claim — verifier would be broken for all such tokens if the mapping code throws on absent key
GitHub Actions OIDC tokens may lack a scope claim but are one of the named target providers
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3 or as a new AC: 'A token with no scope/scp claim → accepted with AccessToken.scopes == [] (not raised, not None).' Also add a variant for scp as a space-separated string (e.g. Azure AD style) vs scope as a list, since both are in-scope for the named providers.

findings.finding: The SSRF private-host guard is described as rejecting 'private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`, RFC 1918, loopback)' but the detection mechanism is unspecified: the executor must independently choose between (a) IP-literal range check only, (b) DNS resolution at construction to catch hostname-based private addresses, or (c) both. These differ significantly in security properties and complexity — hostname-only URIs pointing to private IPs would bypass an IP-range-only check. The AC's observable oracle only exercises IP literals (`169.254.169.254`), so an IP-range-only implementation satisfies the AC while leaving hostname-based SSRF open.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch'; AC 'construction-time SSRF guards: ... private/link-local host is rejected by default'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to the Security section, e.g.: 'The private-host check is applied to the **resolved IP** of the JWKS URI hostname (synchronous DNS lookup at construction time via `socket.getaddrinfo`), not only to IP-literal URIs.' Then add to the SSRF AC: 'a `jwks_uri` whose hostname resolves to a private IP (e.g. a loopback hostname) is also rejected by default.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the time-control mechanism for the 'validates after cooldown elapses' sub-assertion in AC 9 (e.g. 'patch `asyncio.get_event_loop().time` / `time.monotonic` or inject a clock dependency into the verifier so the test can advance time without sleeping').
evidence: 'while a token signed by a rotated key validates after the cooldown elapses (a legitimate rotation still refetches)' — no time-control mechanism specified
The cooldown default is 30 s; a real sleep would make CI slow; a mock requires knowing which time primitive to patch
findings.finding: AC 9's second observable oracle — 'a token signed by a rotated key validates after the cooldown elapses' — does not specify how time is controlled in the test. An implementer must choose between actually sleeping (making the test slow/flaky) or patching time (requiring a specific hook). Neither approach is named, leaving an executable gap.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 9 (unknown-kid cooldown concurrency test)
scenarios: Implementer patches the wrong monotonic-clock primitive and the test passes vacuously (cooldown never fires).
Implementer uses a real 30 s sleep and CI timeout kills the test run.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC 9: 'Time is controlled by injecting a monotonic-clock callable into the verifier (defaulting to `time.monotonic`); the test advances it past the cooldown without sleeping.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly in the Security section that the cooldown timestamp must be written *inside the same lock acquisition that read it, before `asyncio.to_thread` is invoked* — e.g., 'acquire lock → check elapsed time → if refetch needed, write `last_refetch = now()` → release lock → `await asyncio.to_thread(fetch)`' — so the ordering constraint is unambiguous to the implementer.
evidence: 'it is therefore made atomic under an asyncio.Lock that serializes the check-and-update' — 'check-and-update' is ambiguous: does it mean (check+write) under one lock, or (check under lock, then fetch, then write under a second lock acquisition)?

'the last-refetch timestamp is updated on every fetch attempt (success OR failure)' — this says when the write happens relative to the outcome, not relative to the lock release vs asyncio.to_thread call
The plan never states 'write the timestamp inside the lock before calling asyncio.to_thread' — this is the load-bearing ordering constraint that makes the pattern correct
findings.finding: The asyncio.Lock's scope relative to the `asyncio.to_thread` fetch is under-specified in a way that creates a real TOCTOU implementation trap: the plan says the Lock 'serializes the check-and-update' of the cooldown timestamp and that the timestamp is updated on every fetch 'attempt' — but never states that the timestamp write must occur *inside the same lock acquisition and before* `asyncio.to_thread` is called. An implementer reading 'check-and-update' as (1) acquire lock → check cooldown → release lock → call `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` → acquire lock → write timestamp will produce code where two concurrent coroutines can both pass the cooldown guard before either writes the timestamp, both launch JWKS fetches, and the flood guard is completely broken — exactly the TOCTOU the plan says it prevents.
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe)' and 'Timestamp-on-failure policy'
scenarios: Implementer writes timestamp after `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` returns, under a new lock acquisition: two concurrent coroutines both pass the cooldown check before either fetch completes, both trigger JWKS fetches — flood guard broken under burst traffic
Attacker sends a burst of requests with random `kid` values; under the broken implementation, each coroutine races through the unlocked period and triggers its own JWKS fetch
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Key rotation without DoS' paragraph: 'The timestamp write (last_refetch := now()) happens inside the lock acquisition and before the lock is released — not after the asyncio.to_thread call returns — so no second coroutine can pass the cooldown check during the fetch window.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact asyncio.Lock scope for the unknown-kid refetch path: state explicitly whether the timestamp-write (state increment) occurs while the lock is held across the `asyncio.to_thread` fetch call, or whether the lock is released before the fetch and re-acquired for the write, and justify the chosen design's TOCTOU properties.
evidence: Plan states: 'made atomic under an asyncio.Lock that serializes the check-and-update, so two coroutines seeing the same unknown kid cannot both trigger a refetch'
Plan separately states: 'offloads every key lookup / JWKS fetch to a worker thread via asyncio.to_thread(...)'
No specification of whether the asyncio.to_thread call occurs inside or outside the lock scope
The plan explicitly names the TOCTOU hazard but does not resolve the lock-scope placement question
findings.finding: The asyncio.Lock's scope relative to the blocking JWKS fetch call is ambiguous: the plan says the lock 'serializes the check-and-update' but does not specify whether the lock is held across the entire `asyncio.to_thread(get_signing_key_from_jwt)` call (fetch inside lock) or released before the fetch and re-acquired for the timestamp write (fetch outside lock). These two designs have opposite correctness tradeoffs — holding the lock across the fetch blocks all concurrent verifications for the fetch duration; releasing before fetch reintroduces a TOCTOU window for concurrent cooldown bypasses. The placement of the state increment (timestamp write) is unspecified.
findings.location: Security section — 'atomic under an asyncio.Lock that serializes the check-and-update' / Approach section — 'asyncio.Lock (see Security)'
scenarios: Executor holds lock across the fetch: the event loop is unblocked (to_thread runs in worker), but all other concurrent verifications (even cache-hit ones that don't need a fetch) block on the lock for the full JWKS HTTP round-trip
Executor releases lock before fetch: the check-then-act window reappears, allowing two coroutines to both pass the cooldown check before either updates the timestamp
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The lock is held only for the check-and-update of the timestamp (not across the fetch): acquire lock → read timestamp → if cooldown elapsed, write new timestamp → release lock → perform fetch outside lock. This prevents the double-fetch TOCTOU while not serializing cache-hit verifications through the fetch duration. A second coroutine arriving during the fetch will see the already-updated timestamp and skip its fetch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located decision rule for the dual-claim case to the What/Approach section: specify which claim takes precedence when both `scope` and `scp` are present (e.g., '`scope` wins; `scp` is used only when `scope` is absent').
evidence: Plan states: 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes' with no further elaboration of the dual-claim case
No Acceptance Criterion tests the dual-claim scenario (both scope and scp present)
The scope/scp claim mapping behavior is defined only by the single phrase in the What section; no Security or Approach section co-locates the decision rule
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes' as deliverable behavior but provides no decision rule for the case where a decoded JWT contains BOTH a `scope` claim AND an `scp` claim simultaneously. The precedence rule (first wins? merge? error?) is not co-located with the mapping specification, leaving the executor to make an undirected implementation choice.
findings.location: What section — 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes'
scenarios: A JWT from a non-standard IdP carries both `scope` (legacy) and `scp` (preferred), and the verifier silently drops one or produces unexpected merged scopes
An integration test passes because the test JWT has only one claim type, but production tokens with both claims behave differently
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'When both `scope` and `scp` are present, `scope` takes precedence (it is the RFC 6749 standard claim); `scp` is consulted only when `scope` is absent or empty.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the explicit resolution rule for tokens containing both `scope` and `scp` (e.g. 'prefer `scope` string; fall back to `scp` array; if both present, union or prefer one') and add an AC asserting the correct scopes for a token carrying both claims.
evidence: 'maps scope/scp→scopes' — no tie-breaking rule stated
No AC tests the dual-claim case, so an incorrect merge would not be caught by the test suite as specified
findings.finding: The plan states 'maps scope/scp→scopes' but does not specify the precedence or merge rule when a token contains BOTH a `scope` claim (space-delimited string) and an `scp` claim (array). An implementer must choose (e.g. prefer `scope`, fall back to `scp`; or union both), and different choices produce different AccessToken.scopes for the same token.
findings.location: ## What — 'maps scope/scp→scopes'
scenarios: An IdP that emits both claims (e.g. some Entra ID tokens carry both) would produce unpredictably scoped AccessTokens depending on the implementer's choice.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'When both `scope` (string) and `scp` (array) are present, the verifier prefers `scope`; `scp` is used only when `scope` is absent.' Add a unit-test AC: 'a token with both `scope="read write"` and `scp=["admin"]` yields scopes {read, write} (scope wins).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `FastMCP.JWTVerifier` (or the FastMCP auth hook it refers to) exists in the installed `mcp>=1.9` SDK and document its actual namespace/interface in the plan, or remove the prior-art reference and instead specify the concrete FastMCP auth hook this verifier must satisfy.
evidence: Plan quote: 'PyJWT's documented JWKS path and the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.fastmcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED
findings.finding: The plan cites 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses' as design-validation prior art for the chosen PyJWKClient + jwt.decode architecture, but JWTVerifier is UNRESOLVED in every probed mcp namespace (mcp, mcp.server.fastmcp, mcp.server.auth). If JWTVerifier does not exist in the installed FastMCP, the 'prior art' justification is unbacked and the architecture rests on an unverifiable assumption.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
scenarios: JWTVerifier does not exist in the installed mcp SDK version, so the cited integration pattern cannot be examined or validated by an implementor.
If JWTVerifier's interface differs from what the plan assumes, the verifier's shape may not integrate correctly at the FastMCP seam.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `jwt.PyJWKClient.__init__` accepts `timeout=` as a constructor keyword (read the PyJWT source or `inspect.signature`), and update the plan/AC with the verified kwarg name and any caveats (e.g. whether it applies to DNS resolution, connection, or read).
evidence: Plan quote (Security): 'with a bounded HTTP timeout given by the named key auth_jwt_jwks_timeout (seconds, default 10), passed as the timeout= argument to PyJWKClient'
Plan quote (AC 7): 'the test patches jwt.PyJWKClient and asserts it was called with keyword timeout=10 — the concrete default value — so the assertion is exact and unambiguous'
resolve_symbol('PyJWKClient', 'jwt') → EXISTS, but venv file path is not accessible to read the actual __init__ signature
No repo grep returns any use of PyJWKClient with timeout= kwarg to compare against
findings.finding: The plan asserts the JWKS HTTP timeout is applied by passing `timeout=` as a constructor argument to `PyJWKClient`, and AC item 7 makes this an exact, concrete oracle ('keyword `timeout=10`'). The `PyJWKClient` symbol exists in the installed environment but its constructor signature — specifically whether the keyword argument is named `timeout` — cannot be confirmed from the available tools. If the actual kwarg is named differently (e.g. `connect_timeout` or `lifespan`), both the implementation and the AC assertion would silently fail or pass vacuously.
findings.location: Security section: 'passed as the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient`'; AC item 7: 'asserts it was called with keyword `timeout=10`'
scenarios: PyJWKClient accepts timeout as a different kwarg name (e.g. it may be per-request, not constructor-level), causing the plan's security guarantee to be unimplemented and the AC oracle to pass vacuously against the wrong kwarg.
A future pyjwt upgrade renames or restructures the timeout kwarg, silently breaking the guard.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Confirmed by reading `jwt/jwks_client.py` in pyjwt X.Y.Z: `PyJWKClient.__init__(self, uri, cache_keys=True, max_cached_keys=16, cache_jwk_set=True, lifespan=300, headers=None, timeout=None, ssl_context=None, ...)` — `timeout` is the correct kwarg.' Adjust the AC oracle to also assert the default value in the actual installed version.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `docs/env-vars.md` / `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` as an affected artifact in Scope or AC: either regenerate it (if `auth_jwt_*` keys use literal `os.environ` reads) or explicitly confirm it is unaffected (if reads go exclusively through a config helper already in `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS`).
evidence: `.github/workflows/test.yml` line 257-267: 'Env-var registry drift gate' runs `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` and fails on diff to `docs/env-vars.md`
`scripts/gen_env_registry.py` scans `src/rebar` for literal os.environ/os.getenv/os.environ.get reads
AC bullet 2 enumerates `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` and `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` but is silent on `docs/env-vars.md`
ABSENCE: no mention of `gen_env_registry.py` or `docs/env-vars.md` anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate `docs/env-vars.md` or `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` as an edit target or CI gate affected by this story. This is a separate drift gate (visible in `.github/workflows/test.yml` and `gerrit-verify.yaml`) that scans `src/rebar` for literal `os.environ`/`os.getenv` reads and regenerates `docs/env-vars.md`. If any `auth_jwt_*` key is read via a direct `os.environ` call, this gate will fail at CI unless the file is regenerated. If the keys are read only via a config helper not in `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS`, the gate will silently pass but `docs/env-vars.md` will be stale/incomplete. Neither outcome is enumerated in the plan's scope, AC, or the 'regenerates the reference docs' phrase.
findings.location: What / Acceptance Criteria — 'This story registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json + regenerates the reference docs'
scenarios: If `auth_jwt_*` keys are read via literal `os.environ.get('AUTH_JWT_JWKS_URI', ...)` calls, `gen_env_registry.py --check` fails in CI and blocks the build without an explicit regeneration step.
If keys are read via a new config helper not in `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS`, the drift gate silently passes but `docs/env-vars.md` omits the new public surface.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet to AC: 'The `gen_env_registry.py` drift gate passes: either `docs/env-vars.md` is regenerated to include the new `auth_jwt_*` reads, or the keys are confirmed to flow only through the config layer (no new direct `os.environ` reads in `_mcp_auth.py`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a statement in the Approach section confirming that PyJWKClient's cache is thread-safe for concurrent `asyncio.to_thread` callers (citing PyJWT's documentation or source), or — if it is not — specify that a threading.Lock must wrap PyJWKClient access for cache-mutating operations (e.g., on unknown-kid refetch only).
evidence: 'The verifier therefore offloads every key lookup / JWKS fetch to a worker thread via asyncio.to_thread(...)' — 'every key lookup' includes cache-hit paths, which means concurrent OS threads all touch the same PyJWKClient cache
'it serializes the cooldown check-and-update with an asyncio.Lock' — the Lock is explicitly scoped to the cooldown timestamp, not to PyJWKClient cache access
ABSENCE: the plan contains no statement that PyJWKClient's internal cache is thread-safe or that cache reads are serialized
findings.finding: The plan offloads 'every key lookup / JWKS fetch' to `asyncio.to_thread`, meaning on cache-hit paths multiple OS threads run `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt()` concurrently on the same shared `PyJWKClient` instance. PyJWKClient's internal key cache is mutable state shared across all those threads. The plan does not address whether `PyJWKClient`'s cache is thread-safe for concurrent reads from multiple OS threads, leaving an unspecified concurrency assumption at the implementation boundary.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale section — 'Async-safety of the blocking JWKS fetch'
scenarios: Under sustained concurrent load, multiple asyncio.to_thread workers simultaneously call get_signing_key_from_jwt() on the same PyJWKClient; if PyJWKClient's LRU/dict cache uses non-atomic compound operations, a cache mutation during a concurrent read causes a KeyError or corrupted key object
A cache eviction triggered by one thread while another thread is mid-lookup on the same entry produces a partially-read key object

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact constructor kwarg name for HTTP timeout on the installed `jwt.PyJWKClient` (check PyJWT source or changelog) and update the plan/AC oracle to match the confirmed kwarg name.
evidence: Plan: 'with a bounded HTTP timeout given by the named key auth_jwt_jwks_timeout (seconds, default 10), passed as the timeout= argument to PyJWKClient'
AC 7: 'the test patches jwt.PyJWKClient and asserts it was called with keyword timeout=10 — the concrete default value'
`jwt.PyJWKClient` EXISTS in installed env but its constructor signature kwarg name is unverified
pyproject.toml [mcp] extra is only 'mcp>=1.9' — no explicit pyjwt version pin means the API surface could shift
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `PyJWKClient` accepts a `timeout=` constructor keyword as the mechanism bounding JWKS HTTP fetch time — a security guarantee — and AC item 7 makes the *exact* kwarg name `timeout=10` an observable oracle ('the assertion is exact and unambiguous'). However, `PyJWKClient`'s constructor signature in the installed environment has not been verified, and the actual kwarg name may differ (e.g., some PyJWT versions use `headers` + the underlying `urllib` default, or route through `request_timeout`). If the kwarg name is wrong, the timeout is silently not applied.
findings.location: Security section ('SSRF-safe JWKS fetch') and Acceptance Criteria item 7
scenarios: Implementer passes `timeout=` but the installed PyJWKClient silently ignores it (wrong kwarg), leaving no HTTP timeout — a JWKS endpoint slowdown stalls the event loop worker thread indefinitely.
AC 7 test passes (mock asserts `timeout=10`) but the real PyJWKClient does not honour that kwarg — integration test gives false assurance.
findings.suggested_fix: Read the PyJWT changelog / source for the installed version: if the kwarg is `timeout` confirm it; if it differs (e.g. some versions tunnel through `lifespan` + urllib), update the plan text and AC 7 oracle accordingly. Pin `pyjwt[crypto]>=X.Y` to a version where `timeout=` is confirmed stable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the canonical module path and class name for FastMCP's JWT verifier in the installed `mcp` package (or acknowledge it does not exist) and update the Approach section to cite a verifiable reference.
evidence: Plan: 'PyJWT's documented JWKS path and the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
`resolve_symbol(name='JWTVerifier', module='mcp.server.fastmcp')` → UNRESOLVED
`resolve_symbol(name='JWTVerifier', module='mcp.server.auth')` → UNRESOLVED
`resolve_symbol(name='JWTVerifier', module='mcp')` → UNRESOLVED
findings.finding: The Approach section cites 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' as design-validation prior art to justify the chosen `PyJWKClient` + `jwt.decode` architecture, but `JWTVerifier` is UNRESOLVED in every probed `mcp` namespace (`mcp`, `mcp.server.fastmcp`, `mcp.server.auth`). If the class has a different name, lives in a different module, or does not exist in the installed FastMCP version, the prior-art reference is unsupported and the architectural comparison cannot be used as justification.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section ('the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses')
scenarios: Implementer looks up `JWTVerifier` in FastMCP and cannot find it, wasting time or making wrong assumptions about the interface.
The architectural shape diverges from what FastMCP actually provides, introducing an unnecessary seam mismatch.
findings.suggested_fix: Search the installed mcp package for a JWT/JWKS verifier class (e.g. `grep -r JWTVerifier .venv/lib/.../mcp/`) and update the reference to the confirmed name+path, or remove the comparison if none exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (via pip show mcp or mcp's own pyproject.toml) that pyjwt is a declared — not just incidental — dependency of the mcp package at >=1.9, and document this; or add `pyjwt[crypto]` as an explicit dependency of the `[mcp]` extra in rebar's pyproject.toml.
evidence: Plan: 'PyJWT is already a transitive of mcp; pyjwt[crypto] makes the cryptography backend explicit for RS/ES verification. The SDK pin already lives in S1.'
pyproject.toml [mcp] extra: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]` — no pyjwt or jwt dependency listed
`jwt.PyJWKClient` EXISTS in the installed venv (confirming it is present NOW), but this is venv-state evidence, not a declared dependency contract
findings.finding: The plan states 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`' to justify that `pyjwt[crypto]` is available without an explicit core dependency, but `pyproject.toml`'s `[mcp]` extra is only `mcp>=1.9` — no pyjwt version is explicitly pinned. Transitive dependency availability is not guaranteed across `mcp` minor/patch releases and is not a reliable architectural contract.
findings.location: What section ('PyJWT is already a transitive of mcp') and Approach section ('pyjwt[crypto] makes the cryptography backend explicit')
scenarios: A future `mcp` release drops its pyjwt transitive (e.g., switches to python-jose or joserfc), breaking the S3 verifier without a pyproject.toml change to signal the break.
A user installing only `nava-rebar[mcp]` on a fresh environment without the S1 SDK pin gets a pyjwt version that doesn't guarantee the `timeout=` kwarg or `PyJWKClient` interface.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `pyjwt[crypto]>=2.8` explicitly to the `[mcp]` extra in pyproject.toml so the dependency contract is declared, version-bounded, and independent of mcp's transitive graph.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing the implementation, directly verify that `jwt.PyJWKClient.__init__` in the pinned PyJWT version accepts a `timeout=` keyword argument (e.g., `import inspect; print(inspect.signature(jwt.PyJWKClient.__init__))`) and document the confirmed kwarg name in the plan or AC item 7.
evidence: Plan: 'with a bounded HTTP timeout given by the named key auth_jwt_jwks_timeout (seconds, default 10), passed as the timeout= argument to PyJWKClient'
AC item 7: 'the test patches jwt.PyJWKClient and asserts it was called with keyword timeout=10 — the concrete default value — so the assertion is exact and unambiguous'
resolve_symbol('jwt.jwks_client.PyJWKClient') → EXISTS but constructor signature unreadable from snapshot
Evidence class: Partially-verified (PyJWT 2.x docs confirm timeout kwarg exists, but installed version signature not directly inspected)
findings.finding: The plan asserts the JWKS HTTP timeout is set by passing `timeout=` as a constructor keyword argument to `PyJWKClient`, and AC item 7 makes this an exact, concrete oracle ('assert it was called with keyword timeout=10'). PyJWKClient EXISTS in the installed environment but its constructor's `timeout` kwarg name cannot be confirmed from the snapshot (file is outside repo root). PyJWT 2.x does accept a `timeout` kwarg on `PyJWKClient.__init__`, but this is Partially-verified — not Contradicted. If the kwarg name differs in the installed version (e.g., `request_timeout`, or the parameter is instead passed to `urllib`'s urlopen call site), the AC test will fail and the security timeout guarantee silently evaporates.
findings.location: Security section ('bounded HTTP timeout given by the named key auth_jwt_jwks_timeout...passed as the timeout= argument to PyJWKClient') and AC item 7
scenarios: The installed PyJWT version's PyJWKClient does not accept `timeout=` by that exact name → the AC test patches the right class but asserts the wrong kwarg → CI catches it pre-ship, but the implementation fix requires rework.
A future PyJWT upgrade renames the kwarg → silent regression if the AC test is not re-run against the new version.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line inline verification step: `python -c "import inspect, jwt; print(inspect.signature(jwt.PyJWKClient))"` and record the output. If the kwarg is named differently (e.g., the timeout is set via a `headers=` dict or a separate `urlopen_timeout` param), update the plan and AC oracle accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `FastMCP`'s `JWTVerifier` (or its equivalent) is locatable in the installed mcp SDK version, and update the 'Approach & rationale' citation with its exact import path, or replace the reference with a link to the PyJWT documentation if the class does not exist in this SDK.
evidence: Plan: 'PyJWT's documented JWKS path and the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.fastmcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED
Evidence class: Unverified (could not locate JWTVerifier in any mcp namespace)
findings.finding: The plan cites 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses' as design-validation prior art, implying JWTVerifier is a known, usable reference point in the installed mcp/FastMCP SDK. JWTVerifier is UNRESOLVED in all probed namespaces (`mcp`, `mcp.server.fastmcp`, `mcp.server.auth`). This is not a functional dependency — the plan doesn't call JWTVerifier — but it is cited to justify architectural confidence. If JWTVerifier does not exist in this SDK version, the design-validation rationale is ungrounded and an implementer may not be able to cross-check the intended 'shape'.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
scenarios: An implementer tries to look up JWTVerifier in the mcp SDK as a reference and cannot find it, losing the stated design-validation anchor.
JWTVerifier exists in a different mcp namespace or under a different name — the plan's citation is still usable but imprecise.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare in the Security section whether the private-host SSRF guard is enforced at DNS-resolution / connect time (not just construction-time hostname-string check), e.g., by resolving the hostname and checking the resulting IP inside a custom socket factory or HTTP adapter before each fetch, or by explicitly acknowledging the DNS rebinding residual risk and accepting it given that `jwks_uri` is operator-sourced.
evidence: 'Private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`, RFC 1918, loopback) are **rejected by default**' — described as a construction-time check
'jwks_uri is fixed at construction from trusted config' — construction-time only, but PyJWKClient DNS resolution happens at each fetch
PyJWT's PyJWKClient uses urllib/requests under the hood, which resolves DNS at connect time, not at socket-bind time of the original check
Cloud environments (EC2, ECS, GCP, Azure) all expose IMDS at 169.254.169.254, making DNS rebinding to that address directly exploitable if the JWKS domain is attacker-influenced
findings.finding: The SSRF private-host guard is specified as a construction-time hostname-string check, but PyJWKClient resolves DNS independently at each HTTP fetch call — not at construction time. An attacker controlling the DNS for the operator-configured JWKS domain (or exploiting a short-TTL DNS rotation) can present a public IP at construction time and then rebind the hostname to a private/link-local address (e.g., `169.254.169.254` IMDS in cloud environments) at fetch time, bypassing the guard entirely. The plan does not specify that IP validation is repeated at connect time or that a custom HTTP adapter enforcing per-connection IP allowlisting is used.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' subsection; 'Private/link-local hosts...are rejected by default' and 'jwks_uri is fixed at construction'
scenarios: Operator configures a JWKS URI pointing to a domain they control; an attacker who can influence that domain's DNS (e.g., compromised registrar, BGP hijack, or short TTL) switches the A record from a public IP to 169.254.169.254 after the verifier is constructed; subsequent JWKS fetches hit the IMDS endpoint, leaking IAM credentials or other metadata.

In a shared-hosting or multi-tenant scenario, a tenant uses a wildcard JWKS domain where the subdomain A record can be set; the construction-time check passes, but the fetch-time resolution hits an internal service.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Security section: 'The private-host guard resolves the `jwks_uri` hostname to an IP at construction time and pins the socket to that IP (or uses a custom socket factory that re-validates the resolved IP on every connect); alternatively, document explicitly that the guard is a best-effort construction-time check and that DNS rebinding is an accepted residual risk given that `jwks_uri` is operator-configured from trusted config.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Security section statement declaring that TLS certificate verification for JWKS fetches is always enforced (i.e., `PyJWKClient` is constructed with a verified SSL context) and that no operator config path disables it; if relying on Python defaults, explicitly note the dependency and add an AC asserting cert verification is not bypassed.
evidence: 'an `http://` URI is rejected at construction' — only the scheme is checked, not certificate validity
No mention of ssl_context, verify=True, or certificate validation policy for PyJWKClient
PyJWKClient delegates HTTP to urllib which respects PYTHONHTTPSVERIFIER and REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE env vars
Plan introduces no cert-validation AC or configuration option, leaving it implicitly reliant on Python defaults which can be overridden
findings.finding: The plan mandates HTTPS-only for the JWKS fetch URI but never declares that TLS certificate verification is enforced and non-bypassable. Python's `urllib`/`requests` verify certificates by default, but this can be disabled via environment variables (`PYTHONHTTPSVERIFIER=0`, `REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE=''`, custom `ssl_context` with `check_hostname=False`). If certificate verification is disabled, a network-level MITM can serve a rogue JWKS with attacker-controlled public keys, causing the verifier to accept attacker-minted tokens as valid. The plan's security contract for the JWKS-fetch boundary is incomplete without declaring that cert verification is always on and that no operator config path disables it.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' subsection; 'HTTPS-only' requirement
scenarios: A deployment sets `PYTHONHTTPSVERIFIER=0` (e.g., to work around a corporate CA) — the HTTPS-only check passes at construction, but all JWKS fetches proceed without cert validation, allowing MITM injection of attacker-controlled public keys.
An operator running behind a TLS-intercepting proxy configures `REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE=''` and inadvertently disables verification; the verifier silently accepts the proxy's JWKS response.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify (in plan prose or a new AC) whether the JWKS fetch should include a short retry-with-backoff sequence (e.g. 1 immediate retry) before stamping the failure timestamp, or explicitly document the accepted trade-off (30 s outage from a single transient error) so reviewers can make an informed sign-off decision.
evidence: 'a transient JWKS outage delays a legitimate refetch by at most one `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` window (default 30 s)' — the plan accepts a 30 s authentication outage from a single failed HTTP request.
No retry or backoff is mentioned for the JWKS fetch path before the timestamp is stamped.

The stated rationale is flood-guard bypass prevention, but a fixed small retry count (e.g. 2 attempts with a short backoff, stamp only after all fail) would maintain the same flood-guard property while reducing the impact of transient errors.
findings.finding: A single transient JWKS HTTP error stamps the failure timestamp and blocks all re-attempts for a full cooldown window (default 30 s), causing a prolonged authentication outage from a momentary endpoint hiccup. The plan explicitly chooses this behaviour but does not consider a short retry sequence before stamping the failure timestamp, which would maintain the flood guard while reducing MTTR for transient errors.
findings.location: Security section — 'Timestamp-on-failure policy: the last-refetch timestamp is updated on every fetch attempt (success OR failure)'
scenarios: JWKS endpoint returns a 503 for one request then recovers — all auth fails for 30 s.
A DNS blip causes a single lookup failure — cooldown window begins, legitimate JWTs are rejected for 30 s.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'S2 Seam Contract' subsection to S3 that explicitly names the interface this story consumes from _mcp_auth.py: the `AccessToken` dataclass fields (including `expires_at`, `resource`, `scopes`), the `verify_token` method signature, the `auth_resource_server_url` config key type, and the S2 redaction helper call signature — so S3 can be implemented and tested independently once S2 ships.
evidence: list_directory('src/rebar') → no _mcp_auth.py present
search_files('auth_resource_server_url') → no matches in repo
search_files('auth_strategies') → no matches in repo
search_files('AccessToken') → no matches in repo
search_files('verify_token') → no matches in repo
findings.finding: The plan asserts `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` as the implementation target and describes S2 constructs (`auth_resource_server_url`, `auth_strategies`, `AccessToken`, `verify_token` interface, 'S2 redaction helper') as existing seam infrastructure this story builds on. None of these exist in the current codebase. The plan depends on S2, but does not document the exact interface contract (method signatures, return types, module path) that S2 must deliver for S3 to build against, leaving the seam undefined for the implementor.
findings.location: What section; Security section: 'All verify-path errors go through the S2 redaction helper'; Scope: 'on the S2 seam. Depends on S2.'
scenarios: S2 delivers _mcp_auth.py with a slightly different interface (e.g. AccessToken lacks an `expires_at` field, or the redaction helper has a different signature); S3 implementation fails to integrate.
An implementor working on S3 cannot determine what to import from _mcp_auth.py without reading the S2 ticket, causing integration mismatches.

findings.finding: AC7's oracle 'the concrete timeout value is passed to the client' does not specify a measurable test mechanism — it is unclear whether the test should mock `PyJWKClient.__init__`, inspect the constructed object's attributes, or use another method. Without this, the AC could be satisfied by a test that constructs the verifier without ever asserting the timeout argument flows through.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7: 'the JWKS client is constructed with `timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (default `10` s) (observable oracle: rejection at construction / the concrete timeout value is passed to the client)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC7 oracle to: '(observable oracle: `http://` uri raises at construction; private host raises at construction without the opt-in flag; `jwt.PyJWKClient` is called with `timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` — verified by patching `jwt.PyJWKClient` and asserting the `timeout` kwarg equals the configured value).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the complete, closed set of accepted asymmetric algorithm strings (e.g., RS256, RS384, RS512, PS256, PS384, PS512, ES256, ES384, ES512, EdDSA) that the startup-refusal check validates against, co-located with the symmetric deny-list {HS256, HS384, HS512} in the Security section.
evidence: Plan states: 'anything outside the asymmetric families `RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA` on a JWKS source is likewise refused'
The closed symmetric set is explicitly enumerated: '{HS256, HS384, HS512} (case-insensitive)'
The accepted asymmetric set is NOT enumerated — only wildcard families; an executor must independently decide which of RS384, RS512, PS256, PS384, PS512, ES384, ES512 are included
The `auth_jwt_algorithms` default 'RS256,ES256' (What section) implies more algorithms can be configured, but no canonical list of valid values is provided
findings.finding: The accepted asymmetric algorithm vocabulary is expressed only as wildcard families ('RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA') but the exact closed string set an executor must accept — e.g., whether PS256/PS384/PS512, RS384/RS512, ES384/ES512 are included — is never enumerated anywhere in the plan. This is a referenced-enum-without-defined-vocabulary gap: the startup-rejection check (refuse symmetric on JWKS) requires an explicit deny-list, but the complementary accept-list is underspecified.
findings.location: Security section — 'anything outside the asymmetric families RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA on a JWKS source is likewise refused'
scenarios: An executor implementing the startup-refusal check enumerates a different asymmetric set than intended (e.g., omits PS* family or EdDSA), creating a gap in the algorithm-confusion defense
An operator configures `auth_jwt_algorithms=RS384` and it is rejected at startup because the executor's hardcoded allowlist only contains RS256/ES256
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The accepted asymmetric set is the closed list: RS256, RS384, RS512, PS256, PS384, PS512, ES256, ES384, ES512, EdDSA. Any algorithm string not in this set and not in the symmetric deny-list {HS256, HS384, HS512} is also refused at startup (unknown-algorithm defense).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name the file (e.g., `_mcp_auth.py` via S2's registration API, or `mcp_server.py`/`config.py`) where the `"jwt"` → `JWKSTokenVerifier` dispatch entry is registered, so the implementor knows which edit targets are required outside the verifier class itself.
evidence: ABSENCE: `auth_strategies` has zero matches in `src/rebar/` (the seam is entirely S2-created, not yet present)
`src/rebar/mcp_server.py` `build_server()` confirmed existing — not named as an edit target
Plan names only `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` as the implementation file; no other file listed as edit target for the `jwt` registration
Plan says 'Depends on S2' — if S2 owns the dispatch table in `_mcp_auth.py` itself, S3 only needs to add the verifier class; but this is implicit, not stated
findings.finding: The plan states the `JWKSTokenVerifier` is 'selected by the `jwt` token in `mcp.auth_strategies`' but does not name which file (outside `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`) is edited to register the `"jwt"` → `JWKSTokenVerifier` mapping in the dispatch table. `mcp_server.py`'s `build_server()` and `config.py` are the two most likely consumers outside the artifact's directory, but neither is listed as an edit target. If the dispatch registry lives in a file owned by S2 and S3 merely appends to it within `_mcp_auth.py`, this is fine — but the plan leaves it unstated.
findings.location: What — 'selected by the `jwt` token in `mcp.auth_strategies`'
scenarios: If the `auth_strategies` dispatch table lives in `mcp_server.py` or `config.py`, S3 must edit those files — unenumerated edit targets that could be missed by the implementor.
If S2 exposes a registration API inside `_mcp_auth.py`, S3 need only call it from within the same file — no outside consumer update needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the three novel architectural decisions introduced here: (1) the asyncio.Lock + asyncio.to_thread concurrency-safe cooldown pattern, (2) the SSRF guard / private-host rejection policy, and (3) the opt-in typ-check design rationale — so future maintainers have durable, searchable context.
evidence: Plan Security section: 'Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe)' and 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' describe novel design decisions
Plan Approach: 'PyJWKClient's lifespan is a cache TTL, not an unknown-kid flood guard, so this verifier implements the unknown-kid refetch cooldown itself'
Plan Scope: 'Boundary-crossing. Hard requirements (RFC 8725 JWT BCP)' — signals architectural significance
ABSENCE: no mention of updating CLAUDE.md, creating an ADR, or adding a design-doc entry to persist these decisions beyond the story
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions — the concurrency-safe `asyncio.Lock`+`asyncio.to_thread` JWKS cooldown mechanism, the SSRF guard with private-host rejection, the symmetric-algorithm startup refusal, and the opt-in `typ` check — but does not include a step to capture these in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or persisted design document. The Security section is thorough but lives only in the ephemeral story/plan text.
findings.location: Security section; Approach & rationale section; Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A future maintainer modifying the auth layer encounters the asyncio.Lock + to_thread pattern without understanding why it exists or the TOCTOU hazard it guards against
A future story adds a new verifier and inadvertently omits the SSRF guard because the rationale was never codified in project docs
The symmetric-alg closed-set policy is revisited without context of the RS256↔HS256 confusion attack motivation
findings.suggested_fix: Create docs/adr/0NNN-jwks-verifier-security-design.md (or a CLAUDE.md section titled 'JWKS Verifier Architecture') covering: (a) why the cooldown is implemented by the verifier rather than delegated to PyJWKClient.lifespan; (b) the TOCTOU hazard and asyncio.Lock solution; (c) the SSRF guard defaults and opt-out mechanism; (d) why typ is opt-in (provider variance) and when operators should set it. Reference RFC 8725 §3.11 and RFC 9068 inline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC requiring that every JWKS fetch failure emits a distinct, structured log entry (e.g. `logger.warning('jwks_fetch_failed', uri=..., exc=..., cooldown_remaining=...)`) so operators can distinguish JWKS outages from bad-token surges without raw log inspection.
evidence: The plan mentions only 'All verify-path errors go through the S2 redaction helper so no token/secret substring is ever logged' — no structured log event, metric counter, or alert is defined for JWKS fetch failures.
ABSENCE: No AC line requires that a JWKS fetch failure emits a named, structured log entry (e.g. `logger.warning('jwks_fetch_failed', uri=..., elapsed=...)`) or increments an observable counter.
During a JWKS outage the symptom (401s) is identical to bad tokens; operators cannot distinguish the root cause without a specific signal.
findings.finding: JWKS fetch failures are not instrumented with any observable signal (metric, structured log entry, or alert), making it impossible for operators to distinguish a JWKS endpoint outage from a surge in bad tokens without raw log inspection.
findings.location: Security section — 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny)'; Acceptance Criteria — 'Unit test asserts a JWKS fetch failure raises'
scenarios: JWKS endpoint goes down for 60 s — all authenticated requests fail; on-call engineer sees 401 rate spike but cannot confirm root cause without parsing raw logs.
A mis-configured `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` silently causes all auth to fail with no distinct error signal.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A JWKS fetch failure MUST emit a `WARNING`-level structured log event with keys `event=jwks_fetch_failed`, `jwks_uri`, `exc_type`, and `cooldown_remaining_s` so the failure is observable in any log aggregator; unit test asserts the log event is emitted on fetch failure.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` needs to be a public `MCP_ENV_VARS` entry or can be a private constant (or at most an advanced/undocumented escape hatch), given the plan cites no operator scenario requiring deviation from the 30 s default.
evidence: Plan 'What' section: 'auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown (seconds, default 30)' listed as a flat config key registered in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json
mcp_server.py MCP_ENV_VARS currently has exactly 4 entries (REBAR_ROOT, REBAR_MCP_READONLY, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC); this story adds 8 new auth_jwt_* keys
Plan Security section justifies the cooldown value as '30 s' default with no cited operator scenario requiring override
No existing codebase pattern of exposing flood-guard/rate-limit internals as public env vars was found in grep of src/rebar
findings.finding: auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown is registered as a public operator-facing config key in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json (currently 4 entries; this story adds 8, doubling the surface) despite being a purely internal flood-guard implementation detail. No operator scenario is cited in the plan where the sensible default (30 s) needs tuning, and the plan's own security rationale ('at most one fetch per cooldown window') does not depend on operator customization. Exposing an internal implementation constant as a public env var proliferates config surface without a current use-case.
findings.location: What section (auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown config key) and Security section ('at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds')
scenarios: An operator sets auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown=1 to 'speed up key rotation' and unknowingly re-enables the flood vector the default guards against.
The config surface grows with every internal tunable exposed; future maintainers must support and document all combinations.
findings.suggested_fix: Keep auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown as a module-level constant (e.g. _JWKS_REFETCH_COOLDOWN_S = 30) rather than a registered MCP_ENV_VARS key. If operator tuning is genuinely needed for some deployment, document a specific scenario in the plan and add the key then.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the 'PyJWT is already a transitive of mcp' claim by checking `mcp`'s published dependency metadata (e.g. `pip show mcp` or the mcp package's `requires`), and either confirm with a source citation or remove the premise — the explicit `pyjwt[crypto]` in [mcp] already correctly captures the requirement regardless.
evidence: Plan quote: 'PyJWT is already a transitive of mcp; pyjwt[crypto] makes the cryptography backend explicit for RS/ES verification'
pyproject.toml line 82: mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"] — no pyjwt declared
jwt resolves in the installed environment, confirming it IS present in the venv, but this could be from dev dependencies (mcp>=1.9 is also in dev extras at line 156)
findings.finding: The plan states 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`' to justify that the library is available without an explicit pin, but the `[mcp]` extra in `pyproject.toml` declares only `mcp>=1.9` with no pyjwt entry. Whether PyJWT is a transitive dependency of `mcp>=1.9` is an undocumented third-party packaging detail that can change across `mcp` releases; it cannot be confirmed from pyproject.toml or any pinfile in the repo.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`'; AC item 1: 'pyproject.toml adds pyjwt[crypto] to [mcp]'
scenarios: A future mcp release drops its PyJWT transitive dependency; the plan's 'already a transitive' premise becomes false and the verifier breaks in a clean [mcp]-only install.
An operator installs only `nava-rebar[mcp]` without dev extras; if PyJWT is not actually a transitive of the production mcp package, the import fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the 'already a transitive' rationale; the explicit `pyjwt[crypto]` pin in AC item 1 is the correct and sufficient mechanism. Alternatively cite: 'Confirmed: mcp X.Y.Z lists pyjwt>=Y in its install_requires.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the semantics of `PyJWKClient.lifespan` in the installed pyjwt version (read `jwt/jwks_client.py`) and confirm it is a passive TTL that does not bound unknown-kid refetch frequency; cite the pyjwt version and the relevant code location in the plan.
evidence: Plan quote: 'PyJWKClient's lifespan is a cache TTL, not an unknown-kid flood guard, so this verifier implements the unknown-kid refetch cooldown itself'
Cannot read jwt/jwks_client.py source from available tools to verify the lifespan semantics
No repo search returns any usage of PyJWKClient.lifespan to compare against
findings.finding: The plan's Security and Approach sections assert that 'PyJWKClient's `lifespan` is a cache TTL, not an unknown-kid flood guard' as the basis for implementing a custom cooldown. This is an empirically-checkable claim about PyJWKClient internals that cannot be verified from the available tools (the venv jwt source is not readable). If `lifespan` already functions as a flood guard (or the actual parameter that does is named differently), the custom asyncio.Lock cooldown may duplicate or conflict with the library's own mechanism.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'PyJWKClient's lifespan is a cache TTL, not an unknown-kid flood guard'
scenarios: If lifespan DOES gate unknown-kid refetches in the installed pyjwt version, the custom cooldown is redundant but harmless.
If a future pyjwt adds a flood-guard parameter the plan is unaware of, the custom and library mechanisms could interact unpredictably.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
